Notes: Linux- Linux Commands

Linux Commands
Command
It is a pre-compiled , executable program. It is an instruction to Operating System. Linux is case sensitive. All commands of linux must be given in lower case. All the commands are reserved words.   
Before starting with basic and advanced commands of Linux , let use try to understand the File/Directory structure of Linux.
Linux uses extended file system.
Directory Structure in Linux   
We find following standard directories created by system under root directory in linux.

                   /                                      (root directory)
 ---------------------------------
|         |          |             |
bin    sbin     dev     home               (every user has its own directory under home directory)
                                        |                    
                          -----------------------------
                         |             |               |
                     user1      user2         user3

--Every directory in unix like system (Unix, Linux, MacOS) has 2 entries autamatically
  created by the system which are   .    and  ..  
  .    is a current dir
  ..   is a parent dir

~   ::   Represents your home directory. (Directory in Linux means Folder in Windows sy.)
/    ::   Represents root directory.

for directory root,  . and .. points to root only.
Absolute Path 
    -path starts from root dir.
Relative Path 
    -path starts from current dir.

While installation Linux creates some default folders. These are system folders and specific data is stored in these folders. Following are the folders
/bin
In Linux, /bin is a crucial directory that contains essential command-line tools needed for booting, system maintenance, and basic functionality. It contains executable files, also known as binaries, that are essential for running the system and are accessible to all users. 
/bin contains fundamental commands like bash, ls, cp, mv, rm, chmod, and others. These are the core commands that allow users to interact with the system, manage files, and perform basic tasks. 
All users, regardless of their privileges, can execute the commands stored in /bin. 
The /bin directory, which stands for "binary" includes basic binaries that are required for the system's basic function. These binaries are available to all users and are required for the system to function properly. In other words, /bin contains the basic commands that users utilize on a daily basis.

/sbin 
This folder contains system administration commands that often require root privileges. 

/usr/bin 
/usr/bin contains executables that are run by non-root users and are not as essential to the core system operation as those in /bin. 

Initially, storage was more expensive, and /usr was sometimes a Network File Sharing (NFS)share. (NFS) is a protocol that allows you to share directories and files with other Linux clients over a network. Shared directories are created on a file server, running the NFS server component. Users add files to them, which are then shared with other users who have access to the folder.

/bin and /sbin contained the necessary files to boot the system before the NFS mount was possible. 

/etc/shadow
This file in Linux contains user passwords. When the field is unoccupied, it signifies that password authentication is not required for that user. For heightened security, it is mandetory that each user on your system is assigned a password.  
Basic Commands
File Handling Commands
1. Creating a file 
 touch command creates file/files of size zero. 
$touch filenamecreates a file of 0 bytes.
$touch f1 f2 f3 f4     creates multiple files of size 0.
 $touch .f1  creates a hidden file by name f1.

2. Deleting a file
rm command is used to delete the file.  
$rm f1 deletes file f1. This deletion is permanent deletion and files once deleted can never be retrived.
$rm f1 f2 f3deletes files f1 f2 and f3
                                     
3. File copy  commands 
copies file contents with absolute as well as relative path. renames file with absolute /relative path
$cp  f1  f2$cp source destination
copies contents of file  f1 in f2.
if f2 already exsists, contents of  f2 will be  over written,
else a a new file by name f2 will be created.
      
4. File rename command 
$mv command is used to rename the file. The source file will be renamed as a destination file and also location of file will be changed on the disk.
$mv f1 f2       renames file  f1 to f2.
if f2 already exsists, contents of  f2 will be  over written,else a a new file by name f2 will be created. File f1 will not exixt
                             
5. file command 
file command is used to determine the type of a file. .file type may be of human-readable(e.g. ASCII text) or MIME type(e.g. text/plain).
Syntax:
$file  [filename]
$file myprog.py      will display "ASCII Text"
$file diagrams.jpeg        will display image data
$file book.pdf   will display pdf document
$file exam.ods              will display open document spreadsheet
$file songs.mp4 will display MP4
   
Directory Commands
1. Directory listing 
All files/directory from current directory will be displayed.
$lslisting of files in current directory.
$ls  -a   listing of hidden files in current directory .
$ls  -l     long listing of files in current directory 
 
​
Explaination of ls -l in details   
$ls -l    gives  9 column o/p.
column notypeexplanation
1 ddirectory

-regular file

l symbolic link
2 rwx permissions (every file can be given permission at owner/group/other  level.                          Permissions are , read,write and execute)
3
hard links
4
owner name
5
group name
6
size of file in bytes
7
date modified
8
time
9
file name

2. Create/delete/change/print directory
commandexplanation
cd ..back one level
$mkdir dirnamecreate new directory
$mkdir  dir1  dir2  dir3 creating multiple directories
$rmdir dirname  remove directory
$rm  -r  dirname  recursive deletion
$rm filename  removes the file
$pwd print working directory
   
General purpose commands
No
Command
Explanation
1
  $clear
clears the screen   
2
 
$whoami                          $who                                 
Command checks for logged in users   
shows information of the user who executes the command.
shows how many users have logged in.
3
$man command
man command gives us detail manual for each and every specified command  available under linux.
4
 $date                                
$date  +%a                      
$date  +%A                      
$date  +%b                      
$date  +%B             
 displays system date
 day in shortform  e.g  Wed
 day in fullform  e.g   Wednesday    
 month in short form  e.g  Aug
 month in full form  e.g  August
5
  $time
displays real, user and system time
Real: This is the time taken from when the call was given till the point the call             is completed. This is the time that has passed when measured in real-               time systems.
User: This is the amount of CPU time spent in user mode.
Sys   : This is the amount of CPU time spent in kernel mode. Sys is short for                 system time.
6
  $cal 
  $cal  -y     
  $cal  -3 
 displays current month of calendar.
 displays entire year calendar
 displays previous current and next month calendar.

​​​​​​​ Concatenate command  
 This command creates/displays/appends file contents. This is a very versatile command.
CommandExplanation
$cat filenamedisplays contents of file
$cat f1 f2 f3  displays contents of multiple files
$cat > f1creates file by name f1 and gives provision to add data.
Ctrl+d for exit. 
> is called as redirection operator.
$cat>>f1 appends the contents in file f1
$cat f1>>f2  appends contents of file f1 after  f2.
$cat  -n  filename     displays the contents along with line numbers
$cat  fl f2 f3 > f4 data of file f1 f2 and f3 is redirected in file f4
 $cat  *will display contents of all the files in the directory
$tac filenamewill display contents in reverse order.

word count command
1. wc command 
One of the very useful command in linux. The wc command in Linux operating system is used to find out number of newline count, word count, byte and characters count in a files specified by the file arguments.
commandexplanation
$wc filename without argument , command prints number of lines, words and bytes.
$wc  -l   filename     Prints the number of lines in a file
$wc  -w  filenamePrints the number of words in a file
$wc  -c   filenamePrints the count of bytes in a file

grep command  (pattern matching)        
Grep is an acronym that stands for Global Regular Expression Print.Grep is a command used to search for a string of characters in a specified file. The text search pattern is called a regular expression. When it finds a match, it prints the line with the result. The grep command is handy while  searching through large log files.

$grep [options] pattern [files]
$grep  Linux  filename 
will search for string Linux in given file. Search is case sensitive.
$grep   -i  "UNix"  geekfile.txt 
ignore case while searching.  It matches the words like UNIX,Unix,   unix.
$grep   -c  "unix" geekfile.txt  
displays the count of number of matches . We can find the number  of  lines  that matches the given string/pattern
$grep   -l  "unix"  *  
display the file names that matches the pattern .We can just display      the files  that contains the given string/pattern. Here string is searched  in entire directory.
$grep   -l  "unix"  f1.txt  f2.txt  f3.xt
string is searched mentioned 3 files.
   

Advanced Commands    
 find command  
 $ find [where to start searching from] [expression determines what to find] [-options] [what to find]
 Find command is used to find some specific file in the given directory. This command has many options.  It supports searching by file, folder, name, creation date, modification date, owner and permissions. By using the ‘-exec’ other UNIX commands can be executed on files or folders found.
$find dirname         -options                     -command
                                  -name                         -print
                                  -type (f/d/l)               -exec 'input'  
                                  -atime n,-n,+n
                                  -mtime n,-n,+n
                                  -ctime n,-n,+n
                                  -size n,-n,+n
                                  -amin n,-n,+n
                                  -mmin n,-n,+n
                                  -cmin n,-n,+n    
                                  -size n[cwbkMG]
File uses n units of space, rounding up.  The following suffixes can be used:
    b    for 512-byte blocks (this is the default if no suffix is used)
    c    for bytes
    w   for two-byte words 
     k   for Kibibytes (KiB, units of 1024 bytes)
    M   for Mebibytes (MiB, units of 1024 * 1024 = 1048576  bytes)
    G   for Gibibytes (GiB, units of 1024 * 1024 * 1024 = 1073741824 bytes)
$find  ./myDir   -name   country.txt It will search for country.txt in myDir directory.
$find   .   -name   country.txtIt will search for country.txt in current  directory.

 locate command  
 locate command in Linux is used to find the files by name. There is two most widely used file searching  utilities , called find and locate. The locate utility works better and faster than find command  because instead of searching the file system when a file search is initiated, it would look through a database. This  database contains bits and parts of files and their corresponding paths on your system. By default, locate       command does not check whether the files found in the database still exist.
Syntax:
$locate  filename
Exit Status: This command will exit with status 0 if any specified match found. If no match is found or a fatal error encountered, then it will exit with status 1.
$ locate sample.txtIt will search for sample.txt in a  particular directory
    
sort command  
This command displays the sorted data of given file. Data is not physically sorted. If we want to further  process the sorted data, this output needs to be redirected in another file.
$sort  f1   sorts the file.
 $sort  -n  f1    sorting on numeric data
 $sort  -k1  f1   sort on 1st column
$sort  -k2 -n  f1 sort on second column   which  is numeric
$sort  -k3 -nr  f1   sorting in reverse order.
  
Number the lines
$nl filenameallocates numbers to the lines in a file.
                             
Creating links
Linux system gives us the privilage to create hard-link or soft-link for any file.                   
$ln f1 hdlnk creates hardlink for file f1
$ln  -s f1 swlnk       creates soft link for file f1

$ln f1 hdlnk
 This command creates a hardlink for file f1. Here name of the hardlink is hdlnk. It can be any valid  name , just like a file name. This is a new file which physically exsists in the same directory as that of  file f1. If file f1 is deleted we can use file hdlnk as a backup. Data in hdlnk will remain intact.
ls -l will display number of  links for the file. 
Hard link increases reference count of a location .
$ln  -s f1 swlnk
This command creates a softlink for file f1. Name of the softlink is swlnk. It can be any valid name , just like a file name. This is a new file which logically exsists in the same directory as that of file f1.  If file f1 is deleted we will not get the data through this link.
 ls -l will display the first bit as l for softlink.

 Soft link works as a shortcut (like in Windows) . 
uniq command
This command displays unique lines from the input file. The file has to be in sorted order.
$uniq f1 will display only unique lines from file.
$uniq  -c f1     displays unique lines with its count.
                                 
Changing directory permissions in Linux
Linux allows us to assign /remove the rights of Read, Write and Execute to the Owner of a file, Group where the owner falls and also Others. In short Linux allows us to change the access mode of a file
Following representation is used to understand owner/group/others for any file.
Reference  Class Description
      uownerfile's owner
      ggroup     users who are members of the file's group                
      oothersusers who are neither the file's owner nor members of  the file's group
      aallAll three of the above, same as ugo
There are three basic modes used for permissions.
ModePermission
    rPermission to read the file.
    wPermission to write (or delete) the file.
    xPermission to execute the file, or, in the case of a directory, search it.
Following operators are used to add or remove the permissions.
OperatorDiscription
     +Adds the specified modes(r/w/e) to  specified classe(u/g/o/a)
     -Removes the specified modes(r/w/e) from specified classe(u/g/o/a)
     =Assigns the specified modes(r/w/e) to specified class(u/g/o/a)
Following are the standard values assigned by the system.
PermissionNumeric Value
read        4
write        2
execute        1
$chmod 777 filename                 --changes the mode of file for                                                              respective owner,group or                                                             others.
Examples::
$chmod 765 f1  
-here 7 (r w x) is for owner  
          6 (r w no execute) is for group     
          5 (r  x no write) is for others
$chmod g=r filename Give the members of the group permission to read the file, but not to write and execute it
$chmod a-x filename Remove the execute  permission for all users
$chmod og-rwx filename Remove the read, write, and execute permission for all users except the file's owner.
$chmod og-rwx filename same as chmod og=filename
Remove the read, write, and execute permission for all users
except the file's owner.
$chmod u=rwx,g=r,o= filenameGive read, write and execute permission to the file's owner,read permissions to the file's group and no permissions to all other users.

 head command   
 this command  prints  the top N number of data lines of the given input. By default, it prints the first 10 lines of the specified files. If more than one file name is provided then data from each file is preceded by its file name.
 $head  -n   x  filenamedisplays first  x number of  lines  from start
 $head  -n  -x  filename    displays all lines from top excluding x lines from bottom
$head  -n  +x  filename same as head  -n  x  filename
displays first  x number of  lines  from start
                                                      
 tail command  
this command  prints N number lines from the bottom of the given input. By default, it prints the last 10               lines  of the specified files. If more than one file name is provided then data from each file is preceded by its         file name.
 $tail  -n   x   filename  displays all lines excluding last x lines.
$tail  -n  -x   filename      same as above
$tail  -n  +x  filename  displays all lines except  (x-1) lines  from the top.
x is number of lines.

 Process status 
 displays process status for any/all the commands.
$ps process status in current directory
$ps  -e all processess including system processess.
$ps  -e  -o  pid       e is all processess.
o is my option
pid  process id
$ps -eo pid,ppid,%cpu,%mem   ppid process parent id
%mem is memory utilization in percentage
%cpu is cpu utilization in percentage
$ps -eo pid, %mem,sort=%memsorting the processess on memory utilization.
$ps -eo pid, %mem --sort=-%mem sorting in reverse order.
                          
kill command 
kill command  is used to terminate processes manually. kill command sends a signal to a process which                  terminates the process. If the user dose not specify any signal which is to be sent along with kill command            then default TERM signal is sent that terminates the process.
$kill pidterminates the perticular process
      
gzip command 
command compresses only single files and creates a compressed file for each given file. By convention,            the name of a file compressed with gzip should end with either .gz or .z. Gzip is most often used to                        compress text files. By default, gzip keeps the original file timestamp, mode, ownership, and name in the              compressed file.
  $gzip filename  gzip will create a file filename.gz and delete the original file.
  $gzip -k filename        Keep the original file
$gzip file1 file2 file3 pass multiple files as arguments to the command.
to compress the files named file1, file2, file3, command will create 3 compressed files file1.gz file2.gz file3.gz
    
zip and unzip command 
zip is used to compress the files to reduce file size and also used as file package utility. zip is available in many  operating systems like unix, linux, windows etc. Mainly if file needs to be transfered between the server where file size is large or there is a  limited bandwidth , then zip is used to make the task easy. The zip program puts one or more compressed files into a single zip archive, along with information about the files (name, path, date, time of last modification, protection, and check information to verify file integrity). An  entire directory structure can be packed into a zip archive with a single command.
$zip myfile.zip filename.txtcreates a zip file by name myfile.zip
$unzip myfile.zipunzips the file
 Unzip will list, test, or extract files from a ZIP archive, commonly found on Unix like systems. The default               behavior (with no options) is to extract into the current directory (and sub-directories below it) all files from       the specified ZIP archive.
 $unzip myfile.zip

 tar command     
In Linux ,tar stands for tape archive, is used to create Archive and extract the Archive files. tar command in          Linux is one of the important command which provides archiving functionality . We can use Linux tar                    command to create compressed or uncompressed Archive files and also maintain and modify them.
Syntax::
$tar [options] [archive-file] [file or directory to be archived]
Options of tar 
-cCreates Archive
-xExtract the archive
-fCreates archive with given filename
-vDisplays Verbose Information
Commands     
$ tar cvf file.tar *.c   This command creates a tar file called file.tar which is the Archive of all .c files in current directory.
$ tar xvf file.tar   This command extracts files from Archives.

System variables
PS1 and PS2 are system(shell) variables in Linux that are used to display the primary and secondary prompts, respectively.
PS1The primary prompt string that appears before each command.
The default value is \u@\h \W\\$
PS2
The secondary prompt string that appears when a command needs more input, such as when a command spans multiple lines. The default value is >. 

PS1 and PS2 variables can be assigned with different values to them. For example, to change the PS1 prompt to "Ready>", you can type PS1="Ready> ".    
To change the PS2 prompt to "Enter more->", you can type PS2="Enter more->". 
You can display the current value of a variable by typing echo "prompt is $PS1". 
The characters that the shell displays as your command prompt are stored in the variable PS1. You can change this variable to be anything you want. As soon as you change it, it'll be used by the shell from that point on.
